Common Use: Anxiety, sleep support, mild headache, skin calm
Preparation Method: Dried flowers steeped 10 min in hot water; or essential oil diluted 2-3% in carrier oil for topical
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1 tablespoon dried flowers per cup for tea; 3-4 drops EO per tablespoon carrier oil
Cautions and Contraindications: Avoid undiluted essential oil on skin. Not recommended internally during pregnancy. May potentiate sedatives.
Notes from Practice: Grows in bed A1. One of the most useful and versatile herbs in the apothecary. Clients respond well.

Common Use: Sleep, digestive calm, anxiety, mild anti-inflammatory
Preparation Method: Dried flowers steeped 5-10 min, covered to retain volatile oils
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1-2 heaping teaspoons dried per cup; 2-3 cups daily for therapeutic use
Cautions and Contraindications: Avoid in anyone with ragweed or daisy-family allergy (cross-reactivity possible). Do not use with blood-thinning medications without physician clearance.
Notes from Practice: Annual in bed A2. Grows abundantly. One of the first herbs Nana Ruth taught me.

Common Use: Immune support, prevention of upper respiratory illness
Preparation Method: Aerial parts (flower and leaf) tinctured in 60% ethanol for 4-6 weeks; or dried herb steeped for tea
Typical Quantity (per dose): Tincture: 1-2 ml three times daily for 10 days at onset; tea: 1 teaspoon dried per cup, 3 cups daily
Cautions and Contraindications: Not recommended for use longer than 10-14 consecutive days. Avoid in autoimmune conditions. Theoretical interaction with immunosuppressants.
Notes from Practice: Perennial in bed B1. Do not harvest root until year four or later. Aerial parts harvested August.

Common Use: Sore throat, hot flashes, mild antimicrobial for mouth and throat, culinary
Preparation Method: Leaves steeped 10 min for tea or gargle; or fresh or dried for cooking
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1 tablespoon fresh or 1 teaspoon dried per cup; gargle and spit for throat
Cautions and Contraindications: Avoid therapeutic quantities during pregnancy (uterine stimulant). Culinary quantities safe. Do not use strong sage tea with epilepsy medications.
Notes from Practice: Perennial in bed B2. Needs dividing in spring 2026. Excellent sage year in 2025.

Preparation Method: Leaves steeped 10 min for tea; or fresh for cooking
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1 teaspoon fresh or half teaspoon dried per cup of tea
Cautions and Contraindications: Generally safe at culinary and medicinal quantities. Avoid very large therapeutic doses in pregnancy.
Notes from Practice: Perennial in bed C1. Moved one cutting inside for winter use. Strong antimicrobial properties appreciated in winter.

Common Use: Circulation, memory and concentration support, muscle pain (topical), culinary
Preparation Method: Tea from leaves; or infused in olive oil for topical use
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1 teaspoon dried per cup for tea; infused oil massaged into sore muscles
Cautions and Contraindications: May potentiate blood-thinning medications. Do not use in high quantities during pregnancy. Avoid concentrated rosemary oil near face with seizure history.
Notes from Practice: Perennial in bed C2. Mulched for winter. Infused olive oil from 2025 harvest is in amber bottle on shelf.

Common Use: Digestive calm, nausea, headache (topical temples), energy, culinary
Preparation Method: Fresh or dried steeped 5 min; peppermint for stronger effect; topical diluted for headache
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1-2 teaspoons dried per cup; fresh: small handful
Cautions and Contraindications: Avoid strong peppermint in children under two. Do not apply peppermint oil near nostrils of infants. May relax lower esophageal sphincter -- avoid with GERD.
Notes from Practice: Spearmint in bed D1. Very vigorous. Three harvests in 2025. Fresh mint water served to clients in summer.

Preparation Method: Fresh or dried leaf made into poultice; or infused in oil for topical salve
Typical Quantity (per dose): Poultice: enough to cover area, wrapped and held 20-30 min; salve applied to skin
Cautions and Contraindications: NEVER administer internally. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids -- liver toxic if ingested. External use only, on unbroken skin, for short periods. Warn every client clearly.
Notes from Practice: Perennial in bed D2. The strongest topical anti-inflammatory in my apothecary. Always reviewed cautions with B.W. before each visit.

Preparation Method: Root tinctured or dried root capsule; or tea (taste is challenging)
Typical Quantity (per dose): Tincture: 1-2 ml 30-60 min before bed; tea: 1 teaspoon root per cup
How Administered: Tincture; capsule; tea (rarely due to taste)
Cautions and Contraindications: May cause drowsiness; do not drive after use. May potentiate sedatives and anti-anxiety medications. Use cautiously with liver disease. Not for long-term use without professional guidance.
Notes from Practice: Purchased root tincture from Mountain Rose Herbs; do not grow on premises. Prepared custom blend for M.C. with passionflower.

Preparation Method: Aerial parts steeped for tea; or tincture
Typical Quantity (per dose): Tea: 1 tablespoon dried per cup; tincture: 1 ml
Cautions and Contraindications: May potentiate sedatives and anti-anxiety medications. Avoid during pregnancy. Do not drive after use.
Notes from Practice: Combined with valerian for M.C.'s sleep blend. Works well together.

Preparation Method: Fresh or dried leaves steeped 10 min; gentle enough for daily use
Typical Quantity (per dose): 1-2 tablespoons fresh or 1 teaspoon dried per cup; 2-3 cups daily
Cautions and Contraindications: Generally very safe. Mild thyroid effect in high doses -- avoid in hypothyroidism without physician clearance. Not well studied in pregnancy.
Notes from Practice: A gentle, lovely herb. Included in grief support blend for P.H. alongside hawthorn and rose petal.

Common Use: Heart support, grief, circulation, mild blood pressure support
Preparation Method: Dried berries simmered 20 min (decoction); or tincture
Typical Quantity (per dose): Decoction: 1 tablespoon dried berries per 2 cups water, simmer and strain; tincture: 1 ml
Cautions and Contraindications: May potentiate heart medications, digoxin especially. Consult physician if on cardiac medication. Long-term use generally safe for healthy adults.
Notes from Practice: Purchased from Mountain Rose Herbs. Central to P.H.'s grief blend. She has used it consistently for one year.

Common Use: Immune support, cold and flu prevention and reduction
Preparation Method: Dried berries simmered into syrup with honey and cloves; or purchased tincture
Typical Quantity (per dose): Syrup: 1 tablespoon daily for prevention, 1 tablespoon 4x daily during illness; tincture: 1-2 ml
Cautions and Contraindications: Do not use raw elderberries -- cook thoroughly. Avoid in autoimmune conditions without medical clearance. May interact with diuretics and immunosuppressants.
Notes from Practice: Made fresh elderberry syrup with F.O. in October 2025. Very effective for her winter immunity. Store refrigerated.

Common Use: Skin healing, dry or irritated skin, wound care (topical)
Preparation Method: Dried petals infused in oil 4-6 weeks, then made into salve with beeswax
Typical Quantity (per dose): Salve applied to skin as needed
Cautions and Contraindications: Generally very safe topically. Avoid if allergic to ragweed or daisy family. Not for use on deep puncture wounds.
Notes from Practice: Made calendula salve for M.C.'s dry hands in spring 2025. Store in small tin, good for 12 months.
